verses.text: Samuel pours oil on Saul's head and kisses him: 'the LORD hath anointed thee to be captain over his inheritance.' The anointing sets him apart and marks him as God's. Israel's king is not merely the people's choice, but the LORD's appointed and equipped servant.

Samuel foretells three signs Saul will meet on the road — men by Rachel's tomb, men carrying bread, and more. God confirms His call with evidence, so Saul need not act on a bare word. He kindly gives faith handholds, meeting our need for assurance.

Samuel foretells three signs Saul will meet on the road — men by Rachel's tomb, men carrying bread, and more. God confirms His call with evidence, so Saul need not act on a bare word. He kindly gives faith handholds, meeting our need for assurance.

Saul will meet a band of prophets, and 'the Spirit of the LORD will come upon thee... and thou shalt be turned into another man.' God equips whom He calls. Yet Saul's later fall warns us: the Spirit's gifting for a task is not the same as a saved and humble heart.

Saul will meet a band of prophets, and 'the Spirit of the LORD will come upon thee... and thou shalt be turned into another man.' God equips whom He calls. Yet Saul's later fall warns us: the Spirit's gifting for a task is not the same as a saved and humble heart.

God gives Saul 'another heart,' the signs come to pass, and he prophesies among the prophets, till people marvel, 'Is Saul also among the prophets?' The change is real and sudden. But what the Spirit begins must be kept; sudden zeal is not the same as lasting faithfulness.

Saul's uncle questions him, and Saul tells of the donkeys but says nothing of the kingdom. His modesty is becoming — he does not parade the great word given him. A heart not yet puffed up holds its honours quietly and waits on God's timing.

Samuel gathers Israel and speaks plainly: in demanding a king they have 'rejected your God, who himself saved you out of all your adversities.' Even as God grants their wish, He names the sin in it. Getting what we wrongly crave never cancels the wrong of the craving.

The lot falls on Saul, but he has hidden 'among the stuff' — too shy, or too daunted, to step forward. Found and set before the people, he is hailed, 'God save the king.' He begins low and bashful; would that he had stayed so humble to the end.

Samuel writes down the rights of the kingdom and lays it before the LORD. Some 'children of Belial' despise the new king, but Saul 'held his peace.' To bear contempt in silence is a mark of early grace. A leader is tested by how he answers those who scorn him.
